title: Myths, Fables, and Hard Truths About AI Governance | Council on Foreign Relations
gist: The U.S. used export controls to impose a de-facto blanket ban on Anthropic's frontier 'Mythos-class' models (including consumer Claude Fable 5), exposing the absence of any comprehensive federal AI law and signaling that frontier AI is now treated as a national-security chokepoint that government, not companies, will gate.

themes.theme: Export Controls as Frontier-Model Kill Switch

insights.claim: The U.S. government invoked export controls in June 2026 to bar any foreign national worldwide, including Anthropic's own foreign-national employees, from using 'Mythos-class' models including the consumer Claude Fable 5, amounting to a de-facto blanket ban.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Frontier AI access can now be switched off by executive fiat; model availability is a policy variable, not a product guarantee, for any firm building on U.S. frontier APIs.
insights.quote: any foreign national, whether inside or outside the United States, including foreign national Anthropic employees
insights.locator: Earlier this month, the U.S. government invoked

insights.claim: Enforcing a U.S.-citizen-only carve-out for a widely distributed software model is described as nearly impossible, so a nominally targeted restriction functions in practice as a total ban.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Watch for restrictions on software models to default to maximalist outcomes; partial controls on distributed AI tend to collapse into full denial.
insights.locator: While in theory U.S. citizens were permitted

insights.claim: The U.S. is positioning frontier AI as a controllable chokepoint it can open or close at will, explicitly analogized to China and rare-earth magnets and Iran and the Strait of Hormuz.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Treat U.S. frontier-model access as a geopolitical leverage instrument; counterparties dependent on it carry the same supply-risk premium as commodity chokepoints.
insights.quote: the United States has reaffirmed its control of a critical chokepoint it can open or close as it sees fit
insights.locator: Like China and magnets or Iran

themes.theme: Regulatory Vacuum and Policy Volatility Risk

insights.claim: There are still no comprehensive federal laws regulating AI in the United States, forcing the government to regulate AI companies via ad hoc export controls and supply-chain risk designations rather than a legislative framework.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Absent statutory rules, AI firms face unpredictable, improvised interventions; legal-regime risk is unpriced and structurally elevated for the sector.
insights.quote: There still aren't any comprehensive federal laws regulating AI in the United States.

insights.claim: It is the government, not companies, that will determine whether a model or a particular use is too dangerous, leaving private AI industry exposed to policymaker volatility with few constraints on state intervention.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Governance authority over frontier models has effectively shifted to the state; corporate AI safety policies can be overridden, raising sovereign-style risk for AI equity and credit.
insights.quote: It will be the government, not companies, that determine whether a model, or a particular use of a model, is too dangerous.
insights.locator: One issue appears to be settled

insights.claim: The Pentagon criticized Anthropic for attempting to put guardrails on military use of its Claude product for autonomous weapons and domestic surveillance, asserting the right to use it for all lawful purposes.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Vendor-imposed use restrictions on frontier models face direct government pushback; defense-AI deployment terms may be dictated by the state, not the model maker.
insights.quote: for “all lawful purposes,“ including autonomous weapons and domestic surveillance
insights.locator: The Pentagon slammed Anthropic earlier this year

insights.claim: Slow-moving regulatory processes colliding with frontier-model bans could undermine the AI investment frenzy, which is underwritten by the assumption of rapid AI adoption.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: If adoption velocity is the load-bearing assumption behind AI valuations, government-imposed access friction is a direct threat to the bull case worth hedging.
insights.quote: this development could have serious implications for the AI investment frenzy, which is underwritten by the assumption of rapid AI adoption
insights.locator: Given the slow pace at which regulatory

insights.claim: The costs of failing to solve the AI governance problem will compound over time through potential cyberattacks or slower productivity growth.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Frame AI governance failure as a compounding macro drag on productivity and a cyber-risk tail, not a one-off headline.
insights.quote: the costs of failing to implement a solution—be they potential cyber attacks or slower productivity growth—will compound over time
insights.locator: In this case, the costs of failing

insights.claim: Countries outside the U.S. face a binary choice between building on U.S. frontier models they could lose access to at any time, or using Chinese and open-source models that are a step behind the cutting edge and carry added risks.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Non-U.S. AI buildout bifurcates along a reliability-vs-capability axis; Chinese and open-source model demand rises as a hedge against U.S. access denial.
insights.quote: building with models they could lose access to at any time, or using domestic and open source models—largely from China
insights.locator: For the time being, those outside

insights.claim: Other countries may opt for AI sovereignty over serfdom, accelerating fragmentation of the global AI landscape, slowing growth, and undermining the U.S. goal of defining the global AI architecture.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Expect more sovereign-AI and national-stack investment abroad; the bet on a unified U.S.-led AI architecture weakens, favoring regional compute and local-model plays.
insights.quote: Other countries may well opt for AI sovereignty over serfdom, even if the alternatives are sub-optimal.

insights.claim: The U.S. strategy of embedding allies and partners in its tech stack now collides with the risk of sharing its most powerful technologies, creating an explicit tradeoff between alliance-building and technology security.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Allied lock-in into U.S. AI infrastructure is no longer assured; partners may diversify, eroding the network-effect moat of U.S. frontier providers.
insights.quote: there will be a trade off between that objective and the risk of sharing some of our most powerful technologies
insights.locator: The United States has sought to embed

insights.claim: Cited possible triggers for the de-facto ban include Anthropic sharing powerful model versions with a South Korean telecom without White House permission, an escalating Trump-administration dispute over Pentagon-use guardrails, and third-party jailbreaking for dangerous cyber uses.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Any of these vectors (unauthorized cross-border sharing, government disputes, jailbreaks) can independently trigger access denial; firms should treat all three as live tail risks.
insights.quote: We may never know which straw broke the Claude camel’s back
insights.locator: The proximate cause of this de-facto ban

insights.claim: Andrea Miotti proposed an International Treaty to Ban Superintelligence as a draconian solution that would make building the most advanced models illegal outright.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Outright prohibition of frontier capability is now in the serious-proposal arena; monitor as a regulatory tail scenario that would invalidate the AI-scaling thesis entirely.
insights.quote: create an “International Treaty to Ban Superintelligence.”
